evidence.title: National install planning anchor
evidence.note: Broad homeowner planning anchor based on public commercial install ranges. Used only as a starting point, not as a septic-specific official schedule.

evidence.title: National replacement planning anchor
evidence.note: Broad homeowner planning anchor for replacement work, used before state price-level adjustments and site-risk multipliers.

evidence.title: National drain field planning anchor
evidence.note: Broad drain field replacement anchor used for early planning only.

evidence.title: National perc test planning anchor
evidence.note: Broad perc-test planning anchor. The test result often matters more than the testing invoice itself.

evidence.title: National pumping planning anchor
evidence.note: Broad pumping anchor for maintenance planning, not a local service quote.

evidence.title: National inspection planning anchor
evidence.note: Broad inspection planning anchor. Missing records or advanced systems can make the real risk much bigger than the inspection invoice.

evidence.title: National buyer-risk planning anchor
evidence.note: Broad buyer-side planning anchor for inspection, records, and early septic due diligence costs.

evidence.title: Georgia regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: Georgia runs slightly below the national broad price level in the current planning model, so the state estimate starts just under the national install anchor before site risk is layered in.

evidence.title: Pennsylvania regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: Pennsylvania stays close to the national broad price level, so site conditions and local administration matter more than the state multiplier alone.

evidence.title: Connecticut regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: Connecticut runs modestly above the national broad price level in the planning model, which lifts the state estimate before system-type or site-risk multipliers apply.

evidence.title: Oregon regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: Oregon sits above the national broad price level in the planning model, which is one reason wide replacement ranges can move up quickly before site-evaluation risk is even resolved.

evidence.title: Missouri regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: Missouri starts below the national broad price level in the planning model, but county authority and site complexity can still erase that advantage quickly.

evidence.title: North Carolina regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: North Carolina stays below the national broad price level in the planning model, but permit ladder complexity can still move real quotes away from the low end.

evidence.title: New Jersey regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: New Jersey runs above the national broad price level in the planning model, which pushes the replacement midpoint up before management-program or special-area constraints are considered.

evidence.title: Washington regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: Washington runs above the national broad price level in the planning model, so even inspection and pumping planning ranges tend to sit above the national starting point.

evidence.title: Massachusetts regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: Massachusetts runs well above the national broad price level in the planning model, which is one reason Title 5 buyer-risk and replacement conversations start from a higher midpoint.

evidence.title: Florida regional price-level adjustment
evidence.note: Florida sits near the national broad price level in the planning model, so DEP-versus-county routing and operating-permit context often matter more than the state multiplier alone.
